QF.id: XIII Introduction to Forwards and Futures_31

question.text: What is 'reverse cash and carry arbitrage'?

answer.text: When futures are underpriced, sell (short) the underlying in spot, invest the proceeds at the risk-free rate, and buy (long) futures to lock in profit
The opposite of any arbitrage trade — accepting a loss to maintain a position
Buying futures and simultaneously buying the underlying for double exposure
Converting futures positions to options positions to reduce risk

explanation.text: **The Logic:** If Futures < S(1+r×t), the futures are underpriced. Arbitrage: (1) Short sell the underlying in spot, (2) Invest cash proceeds at risk-free rate, (3) Buy futures. At expiry: take delivery via futures, return borrowed stock. Profit = S(1+r×t) - Futures price > 0. **The Trap:** This is a legitimate arbitrage strategy, not a way to accept losses. It requires the ability to short sell the underlying, which may face restrictions. **Cross-Exam Context:** Reverse cash-and-carry is possible in equity (VIII) and commodity (XVI) markets. In currency markets, the equivalent involves borrowing/lending in different currencies. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Short selling restrictions and stock borrowing costs limit reverse cash-and-carry in practice — this is why futures can trade slightly below theoretical without being arbitraged.
